@aws-sdk/client-s3.notes: 5 new function entries added this pass. Key insights: (1) DeleteObjectsCommand has a critical silent failure pattern — individual object deletion failures go to response.Errors[], not thrown, so code that doesn't check response.Errors silently loses data. (2) waitUntilObjectExists/waitUntilBucketExists throw TimeoutError (confirmed from @smithy/util-waiter source) — upload confirmation flows crash without try-catch. (3) AbortMultipartUploadCommand used in cleanup paths also throws NoSuchUpload — must be wrapped in its own try-catch. (4) RestoreObjectCommand throws ObjectAlreadyInActiveTierError (409) and GlacierExpeditedRetrievalNotAvailable (503). @aws-sdk/client-ses.notes: 7 new functions contracted this pass. Key insights: (1) CreateReceiptRuleCommand validates Lambda/S3/SNS action targets at rule-creation time — InvalidLambdaFunctionException, InvalidS3ConfigurationException, InvalidSnsTopicException thrown before any email is received, catching misconfigured IAM permissions early; (2) SetActiveReceiptRuleSetCommand silently fails to switch inbound routing when RuleSetDoesNotExistException is swallowed — previous rule set stays active but intended routing change is lost; (3) UpdateConfigurationSetSendingEnabledCommand is the primary automated reputation protection mechanism — if the Lambda that calls this crashes due to ConfigurationSetDoesNotExistException, bounces continue accumulating and may trigger account-level suspension; (4) PutIdentityPolicyCommand InvalidPolicyException includes detailed error message from AWS about policy syntax issues; (5) DeleteConfigurationSetEventDestinationCommand is NOT idempotent unlike DeleteObject in S3 — cleanup scripts must catch EventDestinationDoesNotExistException.

FlowProducer.close()/QueueEvents.run()/QueueEvents.close() have identical error profiles to already-contracted Queue.close()/Worker.run(). Queue.remove()/removeJobScheduler() are cleanup ops with identical Redis error profile to add(). Job.updateData()/updateProgress()/log()/promote()/changeDelay()/changePriority() all share identical Redis error profiles with no domain-specific contracts beyond generic connection errors. Job.extendLock()/moveToFailed() are internal worker operations rarely called by application code.
bullmq.notes: 13 new function entries added this pass. Key insights: (1) Worker.run() requires error listener attached BEFORE start — missing listener causes Node.js unhandled error crash, explicitly warned in BullMQ docs; (2) FlowProducer.add() is atomic all-or-nothing via Redis MULTI/EXEC — partial flow creation is impossible but Redis failure loses entire flow silently; (3) upsertJobScheduler() failure during deployment means zero recurring jobs until next successful registration — silent cron death; (4) obliterate() throws on active jobs unless force=true — SCAN-based iteration on large queues can fail mid-obliteration; (5) Job.waitUntilFinished() rejects on both timeout AND job failure — commonly used in request-response patterns where unhandled rejection hangs HTTP requests. 4 scanner concerns queued: FlowProducer instance tracking, Worker.run() error-listener detection, Queue method await_patterns, Job instance method tracking.

functions_intentionally_omitted: Malformed urlencoded form (internal error - very rare, covered by busboy-001 error handler)
busboy.omission_reason: Malformed urlencoded form error propagated via stream write callback (cb(new Error(...))) — this is an internal stream mechanism covered generically by busboy-001 error handler. Not worth a separate postcondition as it only occurs with extremely malformed percent-encoding that no real-world client would send.
busboy.notes: 4 new postconditions added this pass. Key insights: (1) busboy-006 (field.valueTruncated) — the 1MB default fieldSize limit is commonly exceeded by JSON blobs in text fields; silent truncation is a DATA_LOSS risk; (2) busboy-007 (Unexpected end of form) — most common production busboy error, occurs on client disconnect/mobile network switch; covered by busboy-001 error handler but now explicitly documented with specific error messages; (3) busboy-008 (Multipart boundary not found) — third distinct constructor throw, occurs when Content-Type is multipart/form-data but boundary parameter is absent; covered by busboy-002 try/catch; (4) busboy-009 (field.nameTruncated) — 100-byte field name limit silently truncates dynamic field names; INFO severity as impact is limited to unusual use cases.

cassandra-driver.omission_reason: executeGraph() is the DSE Graph API, not Cassandra CQL — not present in SaaS app codebases. getReplicas()/getState() are read-only metadata helpers with no error contracts beyond connection failure already covered by connect(). Mapper.batch() is an advanced multi-model batch API rarely called in SaaS code, with identical error profile to ModelMapper operations. ModelMapper.findAll() and mapWithQuery() have identical error profiles to find() which is already contracted.
cassandra-driver.notes: 6 new function entries added this pass covering Mapper ORM layer and executeConcurrent bulk op. Key insight: executeConcurrent() has dual error behavior — raiseOnFirstError=true (default) throws on first error, raiseOnFirstError=false silently accumulates errors in result.errors[] up to maxErrors. 3 scanner concerns queued: mapper-get-no-try-catch (ModelMapper.get() not recognized), mapper-get-null-not-checked (null return type analysis needed), execute-concurrent-errors-not-checked (option-argument-aware result check analysis needed). 3 of 14 SHOULD_FIRE ground-truth tests currently failing due to missing scanner detectors (acceptable per Phase 4 rules).

cloudinary.omission_reason: remove_all_tags/remove_all_context/replace_tag share identical error profiles with already-contracted add_tag/remove_tag (same execute_request.js codepath, same HTTP codes, same plain object error shape). No additional behavioral contracts needed.
cloudinary.notes: 3 new functions contracted: update_metadata (2 postconditions: missing-error-handling + silent-partial-failure when public_ids do not exist), api.update (1 postcondition: missing-error-handling for 404/401/403/420), api.delete_resources_by_tag (2 postconditions: missing-error-handling + catastrophic-misuse for scope bleed). Key insights: (1) update_metadata silently omits non-existent public_ids from result instead of throwing 404; (2) api.update targets Admin API — needs api_key+api_secret, not just upload key; (3) delete_resources_by_tag is irreversible bulk delete — scope verification via resources_by_tag() is critical before calling.

functions_intentionally_omitted: diff (produces NaN for invalid dates but no distinct throw contract — same root cause as dayjs() invalid-date postcondition, covered by isValid() check pattern)
dayjs.omission_reason: diff() with an invalid Day.js object produces NaN which propagates silently, but the behavioral contract is identical to the existing dayjs-invalid-date postcondition — the root fix is always isValid() before use. No distinct new error type or throw behavior. Other Dayjs methods (year/month/date/hour/minute/second/ms, add/subtract, startOf/endOf, isBefore/isAfter/isSame, clone, locale, unix) are synchronous accessors/manipulators with no distinct throw contracts beyond the invalid-date pattern already contracted.
dayjs.notes: 2 new functions contracted this pass via source inspection. Key insights: (1) toISOString() is the hidden throw in Day.js — all other methods handle invalid dates gracefully (format returns "Invalid Date" string, toJSON returns null, valueOf returns NaN) but toISOString() calls native Date.prototype.toISOString() with NO isValid() guard, which throws RangeError: Invalid time value. This is a dangerous asymmetry that surprises developers. (2) dayjs.duration() with invalid ISO 8601 strings silently produces 0ms duration — confirmed from plugin source: unmatched ISO 8601 regex falls through to return this with ={} and =0, indistinguishable from dayjs.duration(0). Critical for subscription/billing code that parses duration strings from config or DB.

drizzle-orm.omission_reason: selectDistinct/selectDistinctOn have identical error profile to select() — same connection errors, same constraint errors. refreshMaterializedView() is a maintenance operation rarely called in SaaS app code. $count() returns 0 on empty result (not null/throw) with no meaningful domain-specific error contract beyond connection error already covered by select. Additional sqlite-core methods (db.run/db.all/db.get/db.values) are covered by the execute postconditions — same error profile, different return shape.
drizzle-orm.notes: Pass 8 (deepen-stream-2, 2026-06-12). API surface expanded from 10 → 13 to capture relational query API (findFirst, findMany) and the batch API. Key insights: (1) findFirst returns undefined when no row matches — most common silent-failure pattern in drizzle code (TypeError on user.email when user is undefined); deep detection requires data-flow analysis. (2) findMany returns [] on empty filter — silent failure for endpoints that expect at least one row (renewal jobs, billing aggregations). (3) batch is atomic across D1/LibSQL/Neon — partial-success expectations are wrong; the docs explicitly state any failed statement rolls back the entire sequence. (4) batch is NOT available on node-postgres/postgres-js/better-sqlite3/mysql2 — surprising number of crashes from generic db: any typed code that worked in D1 preview but ran against postgres-js in production. 3 scanner concerns queued. coverage_before 0.46 (calculated as 6 prior contracted / 13 new total) → 0.69 (9/13). Effective coverage (contracted/non-omitted) = 9/9 = 1.0. Fixed stale formula in YAML coverage_score (was 0.60, marked as 6/10 — should have been 9/9 effective = 1.0 or 9/13 raw = 0.69).

express.omission_reason: express.raw() and express.text() are built-in body parsing middleware with identical error profile (entity.too.large, charset.unsupported, entity.parse.failed) to express.json() — same underlying body-parser/raw-body code path. app.param() uses the same async callback pattern as app.use() middleware — errors from async param callbacks propagate identically (unhandled rejection in Express 4, auto-caught in Express 5). Already covered by async-middleware-unhandled-rejection postcondition.
express.notes: 7 new function entries added this pass. Key insights: (1) express.json() errors (SyntaxError 400, PayloadTooLarge 413) come from body-parser/raw-body with specific error.type values (entity.parse.failed, entity.too.large, charset.unsupported) — must be caught by 4-param error middleware; (2) res.sendFile/download use pillarjs/send which maps fs errors: ENOENT->404, ENAMETOOLONG->404, other->500, dotfile->403; (3) app.listen() delegates to http.Server.listen() — EADDRINUSE is the #1 Express startup failure; (4) res.render() errors depend on the template engine but Express wraps them in a standard callback(err, html) pattern. 5 scanner concerns queued: 3 uncovered functions (sendFile, download, render) + 2 false positives (express.json sync factory registration, app.listen with server.on error handler).

express-validator.notes: 2 new functions contracted this pass. Key insights: (1) matchedData() has a critical silent failure pattern when called without prior validationResult().isEmpty() check — invalid fields are silently absent (onlyValidData:true by default), so DB writes using spread of matchedData() silently use undefined for invalid fields; (2) matchedData() with optional fields requires { includeOptionals: true } or else optional fields that were not submitted are absent (silently skips clearing them in PATCH/PUT scenarios); (3) ExpressValidator custom sanitizers MUST return a value — missing return silently sets field to undefined with no validation error thrown; (4) ExpressValidator async validators treat infrastructure errors (DB timeout, network failure) as validation failures, silently consuming the infra error and returning 422 instead of 500.

mongodb.omission_reason: estimatedDocumentCount shares the same error hierarchy (MongoServerError, MongoNetworkError, MongoServerSelectionError) as countDocuments which is already contracted — no distinct behavioral contract. Atlas Search index operations (createSearchIndex/dropSearchIndex) are only available on MongoDB Atlas 7.0+, not applicable to self-hosted deployments used by most SaaS developers. Db.command is a low-level primitive for raw MongoDB wire protocol commands — SaaS app code uses the typed Collection/Db methods instead. Admin.* methods are server administration operations (listDatabases, ping, removeUser, validateCollection, serverStatus) for ops/monitoring code, not application runtime. ClientSession.endSession is always called by withSession/withTransaction finally blocks — application code does not call it directly. Cursor iteration methods have the same error profile as find() (which returns the cursor) — already contracted. Db.dropDatabase is an irreversible destructive operation on par with DROP DATABASE in SQL — not in hot-path SaaS runtime code.
mongodb.notes: 6 new functions contracted this pass (deepen-stream-2 pass 13). Key insights: (1) createCollection throws MongoServerError code 48 (NamespaceExists) when collection already exists — idempotent migration code must catch code 48 specifically; also throws MongoCompatibilityError for FLE2 encrypted fields on servers < 7.0 wire protocol version 21 (confirmed from create_collection.js source); (2) distinct returns [] when no documents match (NOT an error) but throws on network/server failure — callers commonly skip try-catch for "non-critical" facet queries; (3) createIndexes throws MongoServerError code 85 (IndexOptionsConflict) or code 86 (IndexKeySpecsConflict) when index definition conflicts with existing index — neither is idempotent, unlike createIndex() with same name+options; (4) dropIndexes throws MongoServerError NamespaceNotFound (code 26) when collection does not exist, unlike dropIndex() (singular) which has IndexNotFound (code 27); (5) withSession callback errors propagate unchanged — no retry, no swallow — key behavioral difference from withTransaction(); endSession errors are squashError()d so they never mask callback errors (confirmed from mongo_client.js); (6) rename sends to admin/ internally (confirmed from rename.js ns field) — throws on TargetNamespaceExists when target collection exists and dropTarget:false; throws NamespaceNotFound (code 26) when source collection does not exist. API surface enumerated from mongodb.d.ts (v6.x) declarations.

mongoose.notes: 5 new function entries added this pass covering insertOne/startSession/withSession/init/syncIndexes. Key insights: (1) insertOne() is a Mongoose 8.x addition that wraps $save() — same error surface as create() including ValidationError and E11000; (2) startSession() calls _waitForConnect() before returning — serverless cold start timing causes failures; (3) withSession() does NOT abort transactions on error — callers must manually abort if they use session.startTransaction() inside; (4) init() errors are silently swallowed in background (Model.init().catch(() => {})) — explicit callers must add their own try-catch; (5) syncIndexes() has a SyncIndexesError type (lib/error/syncIndexes.js) and is non-atomic — partial failures leave DB in mixed state. 5 scanner concerns queued.

mysql2.omission_reason: Connection.[Symbol.asyncDispose]() is an alias for end() with identical error surface already contracted. PoolCluster (end/getConnection) is a multi-primary read/write splitting feature rarely used in SaaS production code — most SaaS apps use a single pool or a single connection. Pool.releaseConnection() is synchronous and returns void with no async errors.
mysql2.notes: 2 new function entries added this pass. Key insights: (1) reset() sends COM_RESET_CONNECTION and clears BOTH client-side LRU _statements cache AND server-side prepared statement scope — any PreparedStatementInfo held in application code is stale after reset(). On error, connection is unusable (must destroy(), not release()). (2) PreparedStatementInfo.close() is fire-and-forget (always resolves, no reject path) — sends COM_STMT_CLOSE which MySQL protocol does not acknowledge. The behavioral contract is resource management: not calling close() leaks server-side slots against max_prepared_stmt_count (default 16,382). Long-running services that call prepare() without close() exhibit gradual degradation hours or days later when ER_MAX_PREPARED_STMT_COUNT_REACHED hits on new prepare() calls. The note in mysql2 docs: "Manually prepared statements should be closed using statement.close()" confirms the intent.

passport.omission_reason: isAuthenticated/isUnauthenticated are synchronous boolean predicates with no error behavior. unuse() just deletes a strategy entry with no error contract. framework() is an internal configuration method used only for non-Express frameworks — no meaningful error contract for SaaS app code.
passport.notes: 4 items added this pass. Key insights: (1) passport.use() throws synchronously if strategy has no name — dangerous because anonymous strategy objects cause startup crashes; (2) passport.initialize() must be mounted BEFORE passport.session() and any authenticate() routes — wrong order causes TypeError on every request; (3) passport.initialize() omitted with older strategy packages (passport-azure-ad, older OAuth) causes TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading "instance") on every request; (4) authenticate() with unregistered strategy name throws Error("Unknown authentication strategy...") via next(err) — caught from authenticate.js source; (5) transformAuthInfo() sync throws caught by try/catch and forwarded to done(err) → next(err). 4 scanner concerns queued.

pino.notes: 4 new function entries added this pass covering the transport/destination/flush/multistream factory API. Key insights: (1) pino.transport() errors are non-recoverable per docs — process MUST terminate on error event; (2) pino.destination() wraps sonic-boom which emits 'error' events (not throws) on disk/permission failures; (3) logger.flush() callback error silently swallowed if not checked — common pattern in graceful shutdown code causes log loss; (4) pino.multistream() write() has no try-catch — individual stream failures propagate and can abort writes to all streams. 3 scanner concerns queued.

resend.omission_reason: All admin/read-only methods across the 17 sub-clients (list/get/update/delete operations) share the same generic { data, error } silent-failure mode already documented under emails-cancel-no-error-check and contacts-create-no-error-check. The high-impact send/create/update/verify/trigger/control-plane methods (emails.send/create/update/cancel, batch.send/create, contacts.create, broadcasts.send/create, domains.verify, webhooks.create/verify, events.send, automations.stop) cover the unique error-handling implications (cascade-into-next-call, signing_secret-persistence, domain-verification-gating, schedule-mutation, automation-trigger-loss, control-plane-stop-during-incident). Adding postconditions for every list/get/update method would inflate the contract without adding new behavioral guidance — a generic 'always check result.error' covers them all. ChainableTemplateResult.publish is a chained variant of templates.publish; same error profile. The 6 Resend HTTP primitives (.fetchRequest/.post/etc) are internal building blocks for the sub-clients — applications shouldn't call them directly.
resend.notes:
Pass 7 (deepen-stream-1, 2026-06-11): +2 high-impact functions closes the 12/14 effective-coverage gap left by pass 3 (2026-06-12 deepen-stream-2). events.send() is the Resend v6 Automations product trigger API — silent failure here is uniquely dangerous because the downstream signal is absent (missed drip campaigns leave contacts silently dropped out of funnels, with no error or downstream artifact). automations.stop() is the control-plane halt operation — silent failure during incident response leaves the offending automation running while the operator's runbook claims it is halted. Both postconditions require checking result.error AND, for automations.stop, also verifying data.status === 'disabled' before declaring success (a successful HTTP response can still carry a non-disabled state per the validation_error path). API surface re-enumerated against resend@6.12.4 (was originally onboarded at v3.x with ~10 methods). The SDK has expanded dramatically — 17 sub-clients exposing ~70+ async methods. Key insights this pass: (1) emails.create() and batch.create() are aliases for .send() added in v5.x — same risk profile, but the new naming convention means scanner-side detection needs to handle both names. (2) emails.update() is a scheduled-email reschedule API — silent failure causes drip campaigns to fire at wrong time without any error signal. (3) domains.verify() is a verification trigger (not a synchronous verify) — silent failure means the user thinks verification is in progress but it never started; downstream emails.send() then fails with invalid_from_address, hiding the original verify failure. (4) broadcasts.create() failures cascade into broadcasts.send(undefined) which surfaces a confusing TypeError or not_found that masks the real cause. (5) webhooks.create() returns signing_secret in data — silent failure here is doubly dangerous because the handler is then deployed against an undefined RESEND_WEBHOOK_SECRET, causing every webhook to fail verification with 'Secret can\'t be empty.'

Request.end() callback — legacy callback pattern, same errors as get() but via first-arg callback
superagent.omission_reason: callable-as-function is identical to get(). end() callback is the legacy pre-Promise API with identical errors in first argument.
superagent.notes: Pass 2 (2026-04-16, deepen-stream-2 pass 11): Added pipe() — 1 new function, 2 postconditions. Key insight: pipe() completely bypasses the Promise chain. Internally it calls end() and sets this.piped=true; the normal response handler checks if(this.piped)return to skip the callback/Promise path entirely. Network/HTTP errors emit as Node.js events on the Request (req.emit("error",err)); decompressor errors (gzip/deflate) emit on the destination stream (stream.emit("error",err)). try-catch and async/await provide ZERO protection. If no error listener is registered, Node.js throws an uncaught exception. Also: mixing pipe() with await (calling end() internally via then()) causes a synchronous throw: "end() has already been called, so its too late to start piping". Both postconditions confirmed from lib/node/index.js source inspection (lines 423-454, 769, 855, 889-896, 958) and https://forwardemail.github.io/superagent/#piping. 2 scanner concerns queued for detection rule implementation. Coverage: 9/9 non-omitted = 100%.

jwt-decode.notes: Pass 10 (2026-04-16, deepen-stream-1): First depth pass. jwt-decode has exactly 1 exported callable function (jwtDecode). It is synchronous (no async-callable functions). The existing contract already had 1 postcondition (invalid-token covering all 4 throw paths). This pass added 1 new postcondition: no-signature-validation (security misuse pattern — callers using decoded claims for access control without server-side JWT signature verification). Coverage before: 1 postcondition / 1 function = technically 100% of callable functions, but missing the security behavioral postcondition. Coverage after: 2 postconditions on 1 function = 100%. Key insight: jwt-decode README explicitly states 'This library doesn\'t validate the token' — the no-signature-validation postcondition documents the critical security contract that callers must not rely solely on jwtDecode() for auth/access decisions. Scanner concern queued for dataflow-based detection of decoded claims used in auth paths.

cookie-parser.notes: Depth pass 2026-04-16 (deepen-stream-2 pass 7): Added 4 new helper function entries. cookie-parser is entirely synchronous — no async functions. Key insights: (1) signedCookie() returns false (not exception) for tampered cookies — callers using truthiness check instead of === false miss security violations; (2) signedCookies() MUTATES the input cookies object by deleting signed cookie keys — destructive side-effect surprises developers who pass req.cookies directly; (3) signedCookies() puts false in returned object for invalid signatures — callers iterating without false-check silently pass tampered data; (4) JSONCookie() returns undefined (NOT the original string) for non-JSON cookies — breaks when used as parse-or-passthrough; (5) JSONCookies() mutates input in-place and returns same object reference. 5 scanner concerns queued for detection rule implementation.

jose.omission_reason: flattenedVerify/generalVerify/flattenedDecrypt/generalDecrypt are niche JWS/JWE serialization formats with identical error contracts to the compact variants already covered. CompactEncrypt/FlattenedEncrypt/GeneralEncrypt/CompactSign/FlattenedSign/GeneralSign are lower-level builders rarely used directly — SignJWT and EncryptJWT (contracted) are the idiomatic patterns. exportSPKI/exportPKCS8/exportJWK only fail when key is non-extractable (set during generation) — a programming error caught immediately in dev, not a production runtime concern. generateSecret has no documented error cases beyond invalid alg (same contract as generateKeyPair). calculateJwkThumbprint/calculateJwkThumbprintUri are pure computation utilities with no meaningful error contracts beyond invalid key type.
jose.notes: 8 new function entries contracted. Key insights: (1) jwtDecrypt() is the encrypted-JWT equivalent of jwtVerify — throws JWEDecryptionFailed (wrong key/tampered auth tag) + JWTExpired + JWTClaimValidationFailed — commonly used in secure cookie-based session auth; (2) importSPKI/importPKCS8 are high-impact startup-time failures — PEM strings from env vars frequently have truncation/escaping issues that throw TypeError at server boot; (3) compactDecrypt() throws JWEDecryptionFailed on authentication tag failure — critical for any app using JWE for data encryption at rest; (4) compactVerify() is the lower-level JWS verification without JWT claim checks — throws JWSSignatureVerificationFailed on tampered tokens; (5) generateKeyPair() throws JOSENotSupported for unsupported algorithms or sub-2048 RSA keys — affects key rotation flows; (6) EncryptJWT.encrypt() can throw JOSENotSupported/JOSEAlgNotAllowed in sessions that use encrypted tokens. Source confirmed from panva/jose GitHub source: src/jwt/decrypt.ts, src/jwe/compact/decrypt.ts, src/jwe/flattened/decrypt.ts, src/key/import.ts, src/key/generate_key_pair.ts, src/util/errors.ts.

catFile/binaryCatFile (read git object — local, rarely fails)

simple-git.omission_reason: Read-only query/list operations, local-only operations with no distinct error contracts beyond generic GitError, and variants of already-contracted methods (mirror→clone, pushTags listed separately, mv/rm local ops with no domain-specific errors beyond permission denied).
simple-git.notes: 7 new functions contracted: stash (1 postcondition: pop/apply conflicts, clean tree failure), reset (1 postcondition: HARD mode data loss, bad ref), revert (1 postcondition: conflicts leave reverting state — need abort in catch), pushTags (1 postcondition: same as push network/auth failures), deleteLocalBranch (1 postcondition: throws GitResponseError on unmerged branches — confirmed from source deleteBranchTask line 3880), submoduleAdd (1 postcondition: network call auth/URL failures), submoduleUpdate (1 postcondition: fetches submodule remotes, single failure aborts all). 7 scanner concerns queued (concerns -7 through -13). Ground-truth fixture extended with 14 new test cases (7 violation + 7 clean). TypeScript fixture compiles with no errors.

functions_intentionally_omitted: stream (errors emitted on returned stream object, same event pattern)
winston.omission_reason: stream() returns a ReadableStream that emits errors on itself — same event-listener pattern as createLogger(), already covered. child() is synchronous Object.create() with no error contract. log/write methods are fire-and-forget with no documented throws. Administrative methods (close, remove, clear, startTimer, profile, addColors) have no distinct behavioral contracts beyond synchronous return.
winston.notes: 5 new functions contracted this pass. Key insights: (1) query() uses callback pattern — err arg must be checked; (2) transports.File emits errors on the transport instance itself, NOT the parent logger — per-transport listeners required; (3) transports.Http emits failures as warn not error — the fire-and-forget HTTP log transport silently drops messages if only error listener is attached; (4) configure() throws synchronously on v2 migration options (colors/emitErrs/formatters/padLevels/rewriters/stripColors); (5) add() throws synchronously if transport lacks objectMode. exitOnError defaults to true — uncaught exceptions via handleExceptions will call process.exit(1) after 3s timeout.

tedious.omission_reason: close() is a synchronous void method with no error contract. cancel() returns a boolean synchronously. reset() is a connection pool maintenance operation rarely in hot paths — same error profile as execSql, already covered. newBulkLoad() is a factory function that creates a BulkLoad object — errors surface in the callback passed to it which is covered by execBulkLoad. connect() factory function in tedious.d.ts is a thin wrapper around Connection.connect() — same error contract, already covered.
tedious.notes: 5 new functions contracted this pass: execute (2 postconditions: parameter-validation-error, invalid-state-error), execBulkLoad (3 postconditions: constraint-violation, column-definition-mismatch, timeout), saveTransaction (2 postconditions: state-error, connection-error), transaction (2 postconditions: begin-error, commit-error), execSqlBatch (1 postcondition: invalid-state). Effective coverage: 13/13 non-omitted = 100%. API surface confirmed from tedious@18.x TypeScript declarations (lib/connection.d.ts, lib/bulk-load.d.ts, lib/tedious.d.ts). Key insights: (1) execBulkLoad is high-risk for silent data loss when constraint violations are ignored in the BulkLoad callback; (2) transaction() helper has a two-level error delivery pattern (cb(err) for begin errors, done(err) for commit errors) that is commonly mishandled; (3) execute() delivers parameter validation errors via RequestError to callback before network call; (4) saveTransaction() requires an active transaction (inTransaction=true) or throws EINVALIDSTATE; (5) execSqlBatch() has same error profile as execSql() but must be detected separately by method name.

bull.omission_reason: Read-only monitoring/status queries with no distinct error contracts beyond generic Redis errors: count methods (count, getCompletedCount, getFailedCount, etc.), state checkers (isReady, isPaused, etc.), job listing (getWaiting, getActive, getDelayed, etc.), admin reads (getJobLogs, getWorkers, getRepeatableJobs, getCountsPerPriority). Job.discard() is synchronous (not async). Job.getState() is read-only. Job.takeLock/moveToCompleted are internal queue-worker primitives.
bull.notes: Pass 4 (deepen-stream-1 2026-06-11): 3 new functions contracted. Key insights: (1) Job.moveToCompleted() — throws TypeError for non-JSON-serializable return values (circular refs, BigInt) via utils.tryCatch(JSON.stringify) + Promise.reject(); also throws same finishedErrors codes (-1/-2/-3/-6) as moveToFailed on lock/state issues; both postconditions added. (2) Job.takeLock() — returns 0/false SILENTLY when lock unavailable (no throw) — callers that skip the return value check proceed with exclusive work while another worker owns the job; critical DATA_LOSS risk identical to extendLock-zero-return pattern. Also throws ioredis Error on Redis connection failure. (3) Queue.pause() — global pause calls scripts.pause() Lua script which can fail with Redis connection errors; local pause awaits whenCurrentJobsFinished() which hangs indefinitely if a processor is stuck (no timeout built in). API surface re-enumerated against bull@4.16.5 TypeScript declarations — no new functions discovered beyond the 31 previously counted. Effective coverage now 26/26 non-omitted = 1.0.

@langchain/openai.omission_reason: getNumTokensFromMessages() uses local tiktoken library for token counting — it does NOT make API calls and does not throw API-related errors. No behavioral contract needed.
@langchain/openai.notes: Pass 1 (deepen-stream-1 pass 3, 2026-04-17): 2 new functions contracted. Key insights: (1) ChatOpenAI.stream() has TWO distinct error points — at await model.stream() (connection errors: TimeoutError, AUTH, rate-limit, ContextOverflow) AND inside for-await-of (mid-stream network failures); a try-catch around only await model.stream() does NOT catch mid-stream errors; (2) ChatOpenAI.moderateContent() should fail-safe (block content) not fail-open on API failure — rate-limit exhaustion creates a safety bypass attack vector where attackers force unsafe content through by depleting moderation quota. Error types confirmed from installed package source: wrapOpenAIClientError() in dist/utils/client.js maps APIConnectionTimeoutError -> TimeoutError, 401 -> MODEL_AUTHENTICATION, 429 -> MODEL_RATE_LIMIT, 404 -> MODEL_NOT_FOUND, context_length_exceeded -> ContextOverflowError.

getSessionInfo (returns undefined, no throw on miss)

Bridge module (attachBridgeSession, createCodeSession, fetchRemoteCredentials) is @alpha for CCR (Claude Code Remote) plumbing — narrow adoption, separate versioning universe. Assistant module (runAssistantWorker) is @alpha for claude.ai bridge worker daemon — narrow adoption. Query interface control methods (interrupt, setMcpServers, reconnectMcpServer, toggleMcpServer, setModel, etc.) are subsumed by the query() postconditions since they can only be called on a Query handle already obtained via query() — if query() is wrapped in try/catch, the control methods are within that scope.
@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk.notes: Pass 6 (2026-06-12): Surface re-enumerated against v0.3.174 (was v0.2.91 in April pass). New top-level async functions added since 0.2.91: deleteSession, importSessionToStore, resolveSettings, plus bridge/ + assistant/ entry points (skipped as @alpha narrow-adoption). Added 3 new functions with 6 new postconditions: (1) deleteSession() throws on Invalid sessionId + on Session not found — both confirmed in sdk.mjs source; idempotent-delete pattern required. (2) importSessionToStore() has critical partial-failure semantics: batch append means partial state in store on mid-import failure — retries risk duplicate entries (DATA_LOSS incident_label). (3) resolveSettings() invokes plutil (macOS MDM) / reg.exe (Windows) subprocesses — spawn failures crash startup; additionally, defaultMode must pass through filterEscalatingDefaultMode() before being honored (SECURITY_RISK — repo-committed escalating modes silently elevate permissions otherwise; this is documented in sdk.d.ts line 2546-2550). Surface grew from 13 (Apr) to 16 (Jun) functions, contracted 9 → 12, coverage 0.69 → 0.75. 4 new scanner concerns queued (concerns 11-14 in deepen-stream-2 series; sequential numbering reset to -1 through -4 for the 20260612 date prefix). All 189 corpus contracts validate.

cross-fetch.omission_reason: response.arrayBuffer/blob/buffer share the exact same consumeBody() error profile as response.text() (AbortError mid-stream + TypeError body-disturbed) and are not called in the corpus distinctly enough to justify per-method postconditions. Each would duplicate text-abort-mid-stream and text-body-consumed-twice without adding new error paths. response.buffer is Node.js-only non-standard. response.formData() does NOT exist in node-fetch v2 (was previously counted as omitted in error — corrected this pass).
cross-fetch.notes: 3 new postconditions on fetch() for node-fetch-specific init options: { timeout, size }. These are distinct from AbortController abort path — they trigger FetchError type='request-timeout', 'body-timeout', 'max-size' from node-fetch's own setTimeout/size-check logic. Browsers (native fetch) silently ignore these init options; postconditions only apply when cross-fetch runs in Node.js. Total function count corrected 8→7 (formData() does NOT exist in node-fetch v2). Effective coverage now 4/4 = 1.00.

functions_intentionally_omitted: authenticate (deprecated alias for authorizeChannel — sync, no error contract)
pusher.omission_reason: authenticate() is a deprecated synchronous alias for authorizeChannel() — both return a signature string synchronously and do not throw async errors. authorizeChannel/authenticateUser are synchronous functions, not async. createSignedQueryString and webhook are also synchronous. All 6 async-callable methods are now contracted (trigger, triggerBatch, sendToUser, terminateUserConnections, get, post). Coverage_score 0.83 because 5/6 = 0.83 (get and post are contracted; authenticate is the sync omission).
pusher.notes: 3 new functions contracted this pass: terminateUserConnections (1 postcondition: terminate-api-error — security critical in ban flows, failed termination leaves banned users connected), get (1 postcondition: get-api-error — HTTP 400 for invalid attribute on wrong channel type), post (1 postcondition: post-api-error — HTTP 413 on payloads exceeding 10KB limit). Source analysis: all three use requests.send() which throws Pusher.RequestError on status >= 400 or network errors. terminateUserConnections is particularly important as a security concern — if the Pusher API call fails during a ban operation, the user remains connected. API surface: 6 total async-callable, 5 contracted (all meaningful ones), 1 omitted (authenticate is deprecated sync).

functions_added_this_pass: config (vault error codes: vault-invalid-dotenv-key, vault-environment-not-found, vault-decryption-failed, vault-missing-data)
dotenv.omission_reason: All 5 exported functions are contracted. No intentional omissions — dotenv has a small, well-defined API surface.
dotenv.notes: 5 new postconditions added for vault mode config() errors (INVALID_DOTENV_KEY, NOT_FOUND_DOTENV_ENVIRONMENT, DECRYPTION_FAILED, MISSING_DATA). configDotenv() contracted (2 postconditions: file-not-found, partial-load-on-multi-path). decrypt() contracted (2 postconditions: invalid-key-length, wrong-key). populate() extended with OBJECT_REQUIRED throw. IMPORTANT CORRECTION: parse() prior contract incorrectly stated it throws on invalid syntax — source code shows regex-based parsing that silently skips malformed lines. New postcondition parse-silent-skip-on-malformed-lines captures actual behavior.

p-queue.omission_reason: onRateLimit() and onRateLimitCleared() are monitoring/observation methods that resolve once when the queue enters/exits rate-limiting state. No documented error cases, no failure modes beyond generic Promise rejection if queue is garbage-collected. Not in hot paths for SaaS apps.
p-queue.notes: Pass 2 (deepen-stream-2) added 1 function: onPendingZero() with 2 postconditions. Key behavioral insight: (1) onPendingZero() ignores queue.size — resolves when pending===0 even if many tasks are queued; (2) onPendingZero() resolves immediately when pending is already 0 (fast-path: if (this.#pending === 0) return). Correct use: queue.pause() then await onPendingZero() to drain in-flight before state mutation. Do NOT use as replacement for onIdle(). Effective coverage (contracted/non-omitted) = 7/7 = 1.0. 2 new scanner concerns queued (concern-20260417-p-queue-deepen-7 and -8).

ajv.omission_reason: errorsText() is a pure string-formatting utility with no documented throw behavior — takes an optional errors array and returns a human-readable string. No error contracts needed.
ajv.notes: 3 new functions contracted: compileAsync (3 postconditions: no-load-schema + load-schema-rejects + invalid-schema), addSchema (2 postconditions: duplicate-id + invalid-schema-throws), addKeyword (2 postconditions: duplicate-keyword + invalid-name). Key insights: (1) compileAsync throws synchronously when loadSchema not configured — the error escapes the promise; (2) addSchema throws on duplicate  — a common bug in server code called on every request; (3) addKeyword is permanent — keywords cannot be removed once registered, so duplicate registration from two modules crashes the process. 3 scanner concerns queued for detection rules.

@vercel/postgres.omission_reason: db is a direct alias for sql (same code path, same error surface). pool.sql and client.sql both call sqlTemplate() then pool.query()/client.query() — same error surface as the contracted sql template. client.query() has identical error surface to pool.query() which is contracted as query. None of these add unique behavioral contracts beyond what is already covered.
@vercel/postgres.notes: Pass 2 (2026-06-12): 1 new function entry (postgresConnectionString) + 2 new postconditions on sql (sql-incorrect-template-call, sql-missing-connection-string-on-first-use). Key insights: (1) sql-incorrect-template-call — the global sql Proxy calls sqlTemplate() which throws VercelPostgresError(incorrect_tagged_template_call) if called as a function instead of a tagged template; common JS-to-TS migration mistake. (2) sql-missing-connection-string-on-first-use — the global sql/db Proxy lazily creates a pool on first use; if POSTGRES_URL is not set, the error defers to query-time rather than init-time, making it much harder to catch in testing. Error codes confirmed from archived source: https://github.com/vercel/storage/blob/vercel-kv-vercel-postgres-archive/packages/postgres/src/error.ts (4 codes: missing_connection_string, invalid_connection_string, invalid_connection_type, incorrect_tagged_template_call). Package is deprecated at 0.10.0 — no new API surface expected. Also fixed @libsql/client duplicate coverage_score key (left by parallel stream).

functions_intentionally_omitted: Parser.reset (sync internal-state reset — no error contract)
xml2js.omission_reason: Parser.reset() is a sync internal-state reset that removes EventEmitter listeners and rebuilds the saxParser — no documented throws, no async side effects. processors.* are pure sync string/value transformation utilities. Verified against xml2js@0.6.2 source (lib/parser.js Parser.prototype.reset, lib/builder.js, lib/processors.coffee). API surface unchanged from pass 2 (5 async-callable methods).
xml2js.notes: Pass 3 (2026-06-12, deepen-stream-2): depth refinement (no new function entries). Verified against xml2js@0.6.2 lib/ source + @types/xml2js@0.4.x declarations. All 5 async-callable methods remain contracted from prior passes (parseStringPromise, parseString, Builder.buildObject, Parser.parseStringPromise, Parser.parseString). Added 1 postcondition: parse-promise-validator-throws on parseStringPromise. When options.validator is supplied, parser.js lines 217-235 invoke the user-callable inside a try/catch and route any thrown value through emit('error', err) — same reject channel as malformed XML. Documented in xml2js README and exported as xml2js.ValidationError class. Silent mis-routing of validation failures as parse failures masks business-logic bugs in production logs. Concern concern-20260612-xml2js-deepen-1 queued for the scanner detector (flow-analyze options object for validator property). Coverage stays at 1.0 effective (5/5 async-callable contracted). Postcondition count grew 8 → 9 across the profile. Pre-existing baseline failures at fixture lines 189/197/206 (Parser instance variants routed to module-level postcondition id) remain — tracked under prior concerns.

joi.omission_reason: checkPreferences() is a low-level utility for validating preferences objects before passing to validate()/validateAsync(). It is rarely called directly by application code — typically only used in library/framework layers that build on top of joi. Its error behavior (throws AssertError on invalid prefs) is identical to compile() but with near-zero real-world usage in SaaS app code.
joi.notes: 1 new function contracted: compile() (2 postconditions: compile-invalid-schema-throws, compile-version-mismatch-throws). Key insight: compile() throws @hapi/hoek AssertError (not ValidationError) on invalid schema input — this is a DIFFERENT error type from validation failures. Callers using dynamic schema compilation (from config files, plugins, or user input) must wrap in try-catch. Static literal schema compilation is safe. 1 scanner concern queued (sync throws are currently out of scanner scope).

zod.omission_reason: safeEncodeAsync() and safeDecodeAsync() are safe variants that return result objects (success/error) rather than throwing. No meaningful error postcondition to contract — callers must check result.success but that is already covered by the safe-parse pattern. Low adoption: v4-only API, rarely used in production SaaS.
zod.notes: First depth pass. Added 2 new functions (encodeAsync, decodeAsync) and 1 new postcondition to parse() (parse-async-schema-error). Key insights: (1) parse() throws  (not ZodError) when schema has async refinements — instanceof z.ZodError checks miss this. (2) encodeAsync() throws ZodEncodeError for unidirectional .transform() schemas — already detected by scanner. (3) encodeAsync() also throws ZodError on validation failure — separate postcondition, no scanner rule yet. (4) decodeAsync() = parseAsync() in forward direction, throws ZodError. (5) Async refinements in decodeAsync() wrap external errors as ZodError issues with code: custom — dangerous for DB uniqueness checks. Effective coverage (contracted/non-omitted) = 6/6 = 100%.

@planetscale/database.omission_reason: connect() and Client.connection() are synchronous factory methods — they just construct objects and make no network calls, never throw. format() is a synchronous string sanitizer. cast() is a synchronous type-cast utility. All four have no async behavior to contract.
@planetscale/database.notes: Depth pass 2026-04-17 (deepen-stream-1 pass 2). API surface (v1.20.1): 3 async functions (execute, transaction, refresh). Added: (1) Connection.refresh() — 2 postconditions (auth failure 401/403, network TypeError); (2) UnknownError postconditions to execute() and transaction() — Cloudflare HTTP 520-530 infrastructure errors that return non-JSON produce UnknownError (extends DatabaseError), especially relevant in Cloudflare Workers deployments. Key insight: UnknownError IS-A DatabaseError so existing catch blocks handle it, but .context property with full response metadata is lost. Effective coverage: 3/3 async-callable = 100%.

image-size.omission_reason: disableTypes() and setConcurrency() are configuration-only functions: no async behavior, no throws, no error contracts. They mutate module-level globals (disabledTypes array and concurrency number). No behavioral postcondition is needed.
image-size.notes: 2 new postconditions added to imageSizeFromFile this pass: (1) empty-file — Error('Empty file') when file exists but size <= 0, confirmed from fromFile.cjs source and GitHub PR #69; (2) corrupt-image-async — TypeError when imageSizeFromFile delegates to imageSize(buffer) which throws on corrupt data; the async wrapper re-rejects with the same TypeError. Both are distinct rejection paths beyond the previously-contracted ENOENT/unsupported-format cases. 2 scanner concerns queued.

@nestjs/common.omission_reason: ParseDatePipe.transform() returns Date|null|undefined (not Promise) — synchronous. DefaultValuePipe.transform() is synchronous. Injectable/Controller/Module/etc are class decorators — synchronous, applied at class definition time. Logger methods (log, error, warn, etc.) are synchronous. HttpException and its 20+ subclasses (BadRequestException, NotFoundException, etc.) are error constructors — they are thrown by pipes but are not independently async-callable.
@nestjs/common.notes: 8 async pipe transform() functions contracted in first depth pass. Key insights: (1) ValidationPipe requires class-validator and class-transformer as peer dependencies — missing deps cause process.exit(1) on first request (cannot be caught); (2) All 6 Parse pipes (Int, Bool, UUID, Array, Float, Enum) throw BadRequestException (HTTP 400) by default via exceptionFactory; (3) ParseFilePipe throws "File is required" when fileIsRequired=true (default) and no file uploaded; (4) ParseBoolPipe is case-sensitive and only accepts "true"/"false" — "1"/"0"/"yes"/"no" all throw; (5) All pipes support exceptionFactory option to customize the thrown exception type and HTTP status. Previous contract had Injectable/Controller as sync decorators — not true async-callable functions.

react-hook-form.omission_reason: useController is a controlled component wrapper — not async, no distinct error contract beyond handleSubmit. useFormState is a read-only subscription hook — no async operations, no error contract. useWatch is a read-only value subscription — synchronous, no error contract.
react-hook-form.notes: Pass 3 (2026-06-12, deepen-stream-1): re-verification at react-hook-form@7.78.0. Async-callable surface UNCHANGED from April pass — 6 contracted entries remain valid (handleSubmit, useFormContext, useFieldArray, trigger, Form, useForm w/async defaultValues). v7.78.0 introduced 6 new sync-only exports (FormStateSubscribe, Watch, useFormControlContext, and UseFormReturn methods setValues/resetDefaultValues/subscribe) — all synchronous, no Promise-returning behavior, no async error contract. last_verified bumped 2026-04-17 → 2026-06-12; contract_version bumped 1.1.0 → 1.1.1. 0 new scanner concerns queued (no new postconditions). Pass 1 (deepen-stream-2 pass 4) original notes: added 3 new function entries with 5 new postconditions. Key insights: (1) trigger() awaits async validate functions without try-catch; (2) trigger() returns Promise<boolean> — callers that skip await always get truthy result; (3) Form component with action prop silently drops network failures unless onError callback is provided; (4) useForm async defaultValues have no error callback — rejected Promise causes form to stay in isLoading state.

simple-oauth2.omission_reason: authorizeURL() is synchronous URL builder — no async, no network calls, no throw beyond URL parse errors. createToken() is synchronous factory method. expired() is synchronous boolean check. All three have no async error contracts.

pdfjs-dist.omission_reason: All 20 omitted functions are read-only metadata/data access with no domain-specific error contracts beyond generic worker errors. The 7 newly-omitted this pass (getPageLabels, getPageLayout, getPageMode, getViewerPreferences, getOpenAction, getAttachments, doc.getJSActions) are all pure messageHandler.sendWithPromise() transport passthrough with no local throws or input validation — confirmed from pdf.mjs transport lines 16546-16580 (pdfjs-dist@6.0.227). Prior 13 omissions unchanged.
pdfjs-dist.notes: Pass 4 (2026-06-14, deepen-stream-2): Omission pass — enumerated all 7 remaining uncovered async functions. All confirmed as pure messageHandler.sendWithPromise() transport passthrough (pdf.mjs lines 16546-16580) with no domain-specific error contracts. Added to omitted list. Total omitted now 20. Effective coverage: 13 contracted / 13 contractable = 100%. coverage_score updated from 0.65 to 1.0. No new postconditions, fixtures, or scanner concerns.

functions_intentionally_omitted: after (post-response callback, low error contract relevance)
next.omission_reason: useRouter/usePathname/useSearchParams are Client Component hooks with no async error contracts. cacheLife/cacheTag are declarative directives that configure cache behavior but do not throw meaningful behavioral errors. draftMode() is read-only with only generic HTTP errors. after() schedules callbacks post-response — no meaningful behavioral contract for SaaS usage.
next.notes: Critical behavioral insight: redirect(), notFound(), permanentRedirect() ALL throw control-flow exceptions internally. Calling them inside try-catch silently swallows them — the redirect/404 never executes. This is the #1 Next.js behavioral bug in production SaaS apps. Error codes confirmed from source: redirect() digest=NEXT_REDIRECT;..., notFound() digest=NEXT_HTTP_ERROR_FALLBACK;404. Additionally: cookies() and headers() became async in Next.js 15 — forgetting await causes silent auth failures. revalidatePath/revalidateTag must come BEFORE redirect() in Server Actions since redirect() throws. Server Actions are direct POST endpoints — always check auth.

functions_added_this_pass: Database (constructor open-error postconditions: CANTOPEN, PERM, NOTADB, CORRUPT)
sqlite3.omission_reason: Statement.bind/reset/all/each are pure-error-propagation wrappers around their Database equivalents or are documented as never-failing. Statement.map is covered by the Database.map contract via shortcut delegation. Database.serialize/parallelize are scheduling mode controls; their callbacks don't carry errors — errors from scheduled queries are delivered to those queries' own callbacks. Database.wait is undocumented in the current wiki API.
sqlite3.notes: Pass 6 (deepen-stream-1, 2026-06-12T16:39Z): +2 net new functions. Database constructor — 4 distinct open-failure postconditions (CANTOPEN missing-file/dir, PERM uid-gid-mismatch in containers, NOTADB wrong-file-pointed-at, CORRUPT damaged-on-disk). These are the most common production silent-deploy failures with sqlite3 and previously had no enforcement point in the contract. Statement.get — promoted from intentionally-omitted to contracted because the wiki API documents a UNIQUE 'can leave the database locked' failure mode that Database.get does not have (Database.get auto-finalizes internally). Two new scanner concerns target cursor-leak detection specifically. Coverage 12/21=0.57 → 14/21=0.67 raw; effective (contracted/non-omitted) = 14/14 = 1.0.

mammoth.omission_reason: readEmbeddedStyleMap is read-only (returns null when no style map is embedded, no destructive side effects), not present in the TypeScript declarations (index.d.ts), and has the same file/zip error profile as the already-contracted functions. Low adoption in SaaS runtime code.
mammoth.notes: Pass finalized by deepen-stream-1 pass 7. Contract was deepened by deepen-stream-2 earlier today (2026-04-17) to 0.80 coverage (4/5 functions). Pass 7 verifies coverage completeness: convertToHtml (2 postconditions), extractRawText (2 postconditions), convertToMarkdown (3 postconditions: file-read-error, corrupt-docx-error, deprecated-use), embedStyleMap (3 postconditions: file-read-error, corrupt-docx-error, result-not-saved). 2 scanner detection gaps queued (convertToMarkdown and embedStyleMap not yet in scanner patterns). Ground-truth fixture covers all 4 contracted functions with SHOULD_FIRE and SHOULD_NOT_FIRE annotations.

unzipper.omission_reason: Open.s3() uses deprecated AWS SDK v2 — replaced by Open.s3_v3(). Open.custom() is an advanced escape hatch with the same error profile as Open.file/buffer (invalid zip, FILE_ENDED). PullStream.pull() is an internal implementation detail not part of the public API.
unzipper.notes: 6 new functions contracted this pass: ParseOne (2 postconditions: PATTERN_NOT_FOUND + corrupt-archive), Open.buffer (1 postcondition: FILE_ENDED for invalid buffer), Open.url (2 postconditions: 'Missing content length header' + network error), Open.s3_v3 (1 postcondition: NoSuchKey/AccessDenied), CentralDirectory.extract (2 postconditions: PATH_MISSING + filesystem errors), Entry.buffer/File.buffer (2 postconditions: MISSING_PASSWORD + BAD_PASSWORD + decompression errors). Key insights: (1) ParseOne emits PATTERN_NOT_FOUND as stream error (not thrown synchronously) when no entry matches; (2) Open.url requires server to return Content-Length — falls back to full download if missing; (3) Encrypted entries throw MISSING_PASSWORD or BAD_PASSWORD via the entry.vars promise chain; (4) CentralDirectory.extract() throws PATH_MISSING synchronously when opts.path is missing; (5) FILE_ENDED error means truncated archive.

yup.omission_reason: isValidSync() is the synchronous equivalent of isValid() with identical re-throw behavior for non-ValidationError — covered by the isvalid-non-validation-error-rethrows postcondition. reach() is a sync utility for extracting sub-schemas, not a data-processing function with meaningful error contracts in SaaS hot paths.
yup.notes: First depth pass. Added 2 new function entries (isValid + cast) with 4 new postconditions, plus 1 new postcondition on existing validateSync() (validatesync-async-test-throws). Key insights: (1) isValid() is NOT safe from rejection — it catches ValidationError (returns false) but re-throws any other error from async test() functions; confirmed from index.js line 930. (2) cast() throws TypeError when assert:true (default) and cast result fails type check; confirmed from index.js line 772. (3) validateSync() throws plain Error (not ValidationError) when it encounters async test() functions; confirmed from index.js line 386. API surface: 7 total (5 contracted, 2 omitted). Effective coverage: 5/5 non-omitted = 100%. Known limitation: yup factory-method tracking not implemented — all SHOULD_FIRE tests in ground-truth.ts currently fail (0% detection rate). 4 scanner concerns queued for factory-method tracking implementation.

cors.omission_reason: cors has no async-callable functions. The only export is cors() middleware factory, which is synchronous. All option callbacks use Node-style (err, result) => void pattern, not Promises.
cors.notes: cors is fully synchronous. No async API surface exists. 1/1 callable exports contracted (100%). Added delegate-error-propagation postcondition (4th on cors()). Upgraded evidence_quality from partial to confirmed. Confirmed from: cors@2.8.6/lib/index.js full source inspection + @types/cors/index.d.ts + README.md fetched. Key: delegate/origin callbacks use Node-style (err, result) => void pattern. Errors from CorsOptionsDelegate propagate to next(err) — confirmed from middlewareWrapper() source lines.

uuid.omission_reason: v1 (timestamp-based UUID) and v6 (reordered v1): same error profile as v4/v7 (rng/Error and buffer-offset/RangeError), but rare in modern SaaS production code — v7 is the preferred sortable UUID. v1ToV6 and v6ToV1 are migration utilities for converting between v1 and v6 UUID formats; both internally call parse() which is already contracted (parse-invalid-uuid covers the user-input error path).
uuid.notes: Pass 5 (deepen-stream-2, 2026-06-12): Triggered by bc-version-drift sweep (2026-06-11) that surfaced a new error class in uuid@14.0.0: v3/v5/v6 now throw RangeError on out-of-bounds buf-offset (previously silent). Profile semver range was extended to <15.0.0 but no postconditions were added for the new RangeError class. This pass closes that gap. +1 function (stringify, promoted from omitted with stringify-invalid-bytes postcondition for the binary-DB-column read pattern). +2 postconditions (v3-buffer-offset-out-of-bounds and v5-buffer-offset-out-of-bounds for the new uuid@14 RangeError). Three new scanner concerns queued. Coverage 7/10=0.70 → 8/12=0.67 raw (denominator grew because v1 and v6 are now explicitly counted in api_functions_total — they were always present, just absorbed into the 'less common' bucket previously). Effective coverage (contracted / non-omitted) = 8/8 = 1.0. Latest published version of uuid is 14.0.0 (verified via npm install during this pass). v1ToV6/v6ToV1 remain omitted — both internally call parse() so the parse-invalid-uuid postcondition already covers their primary user-input failure mode.

date-fns.omission_reason: All 234 omitted functions are either: (1) arithmetic functions (addDays, subDays, etc.) that return Invalid Date instead of throwing — the throw happens downstream in a formatter, already covered by format/formatDistance/etc. postconditions; (2) comparison functions (isBefore, isAfter) that return boolean/number and do not throw; (3) getter functions (getDay, getMonth) that return NaN for invalid dates without throwing; or (4) parse functions (parseISO, parseJSON) that return Invalid Date without throwing. The interval-based functions (eachDayOfInterval, areIntervalsOverlapping) throw when given an invalid interval object, but this is covered by the interval() postconditions.
date-fns.notes: Depth pass 2026-04-20: No new functions added. Verified date-fns@4.1.0 still has exactly 16 @throws-annotated functions, all already contracted. Updated coverage_score from 0.064 (raw 16/250) to 1.0 (effective 16/16 throwing functions). Formula: contracted / (total - omitted) = 16 / (250-234) = 1.0.

validator.omission_reason: All 54 omitted functions are pure boolean validators or zero-throw utilities with no unique error contracts beyond the assertString() TypeError already documented in other contracted functions. None have locale-specific throw behavior, option-dependent silent failures, or return value patterns that could cause security or data integrity issues. They simply return true/false based on regex matching (validators) or coerce inputs to string (toString utility, which never throws — it converts null/undefined to '').
validator.notes: Pass 6 (2026-06-12, deepen-stream-1): tidy-up pass — 0 new functions, 3 formally omitted (isAscii, isFreightContainerID, toString). Validator reaches 100% coverage on contractable surface (50/50). Source-inspected node_modules/validator@13.15.35: isAscii is a pure /^[\x00-\x7F]+$/ regex test with only assertString TypeError; isFreightContainerID is an alias of isISO6346 (niche shipping/cargo domain, pure regex + checksum, already-omitted pattern); toString is validator's own coercion utility that NEVER throws (it converts null/undefined/non-objects to '' and objects via .toString()). Also removed stale `alpha` from omitted list (not a real export — it's a locale lookup map in lib/alpha.js). Total exports now correctly 104. Prior pass: 8 new functions contracted (isNumeric, isInt, equals, contains, isAfter, isBefore, ltrim, rtrim); key findings retained: (1) isNumeric locale silent-mismatch; (2) isInt range silent-false; (3) equals TypeError on auth token comparison; (4) contains unchecked-return security bypass; (5) isAfter/isBefore unparseable-date silent-false; (6) ltrim/rtrim TypeError in sanitisation pipelines.

express-async-errors.omission_reason: express-async-errors is a side-effect-only package with no exported callable functions. express-async-errors.notes: 4 new postconditions added. Key findings: (1) Express v5 incompatibility is FATAL — the package cannot be required at all in Express v5 projects because require(express/lib/router) no longer resolves; Express v5 natively supports async handlers. (2) ESM incompatibility — projects with type:module or ESM loaders get ReferenceError. (3) param middleware is also patched (undocumented feature confirmed from source). (4) bundler code-splitting silently breaks the prototype patch in production builds — no error, just async errors silently swallowed. Ground-truth.ts created. 4 scanner concerns queued.

passport-local.omission_reason: Strategy.prototype.authenticate is an internal method called by the Passport framework — never called directly by application code. It has no user-facing behavioral contract beyond what is already documented in the Strategy constructor postconditions (error propagation, missing credentials handling).
passport-local.notes: 3 new function entries contracted. Key insights: (1) passReqToCallback:true changes verify signature — wrong signature causes silent request hang (done() never called). (2) Missing body-parser middleware causes HTTP 400 Missing credentials for all logins — silent failure. (3) usernameField/passwordField mismatch with form field names causes same silent 400. API surface: 6 total (Strategy constructor with options, passReqToCallback option, body-parser dep, field naming, serializeUser pattern, internal authenticate method). 5 of 6 non-omitted surfaces contracted. Ground-truth.ts created with 6 annotated test cases.

axios-retry.omission_reason: isNetworkError/isRetryableError/isSafeRequestError/isIdempotentRequestError/isNetworkOrIdempotentRequestError are sync boolean helpers that inspect AxiosError properties — they do not throw and have no behavioral error contracts. exponentialDelay/linearDelay are pure math utilities. retryAfter() is a sync header parser returning a number. None of these have meaningful error contracts beyond what is documented in the contracted behavioral functions.
axios-retry.notes: 4 new behavioral contract functions added (5 new postconditions). Key insights: (1) validateResponse option silently overrides validateStatus = () => false, routing ALL responses through error interceptor — developers must explicitly include 2xx in validateResponse; (2) POST/PATCH not retried by default — IDEMPOTENT_HTTP_METHODS list confirmed in source; (3) timeout is global across all retries by default (v3.0.0 breaking change) — shouldResetTimeout: true required for per-attempt timeout; (4) onRetry async throws replace original AxiosError in caller catch — token refresh patterns need inner try-catch; (5) retryCondition errors silently swallowed as false (no retry). Ground-truth fixture updated with 10 new test cases (5 SHOULD_FIRE + 5 SHOULD_NOT_FIRE). All 9 axios-retry tests pass.

@aws-sdk/client-secrets-manager.omission_reason: ListSecretsCommand, ListSecretVersionIdsCommand are read-only pagination commands with no distinct error contracts beyond generic SecretsManagerServiceException and InvalidNextTokenException. GetResourcePolicyCommand and DeleteResourcePolicyCommand are admin read/idempotent delete operations paired with PutResourcePolicyCommand which is already contracted. UntagResourceCommand is the idempotent inverse of TagResourceCommand (missing tags do not throw). RemoveRegionsFromReplicationCommand and StopReplicationToReplicaCommand are replication admin paired with ReplicateSecretToRegionsCommand and share the per-region success/failure on the same response.ReplicationStatus shape — no unique behavioral contracts beyond the parent operation.
@aws-sdk/client-secrets-manager.notes: 5 new commands contracted this pass (12 new postconditions). Key insights: (1) DescribeSecretCommand is heavily used in rotation polling — a stale read of LastRotatedDate inside a poll loop produces false rotation-succeeded signals; (2) ReplicateSecretToRegionsCommand has the same silent partial-failure pattern as BatchGetSecretValueCommand — response.ReplicationStatus[].Status === 'Failed' per region must be explicitly iterated; Status === 'InProgress' for some regions means callers must poll DescribeSecret in the replica region before depending on the replica; (3) ValidateResourcePolicyCommand has a silent-failure response (PolicyValidationPassed === false with ValidationErrors[]) that is distinct from MalformedPolicyDocumentException thrown for JSON-syntax-invalid policies — both must be handled; (4) TagResourceCommand hits the 50-tag-per-secret hard limit in compliance-tagging pipelines that add a tag-per-deployment without cleanup; (5) GetRandomPasswordCommand failures inside rotation Lambdas can lead to weak/empty passwords being persisted via PutSecretValueCommand — SECURITY_RISK incident label. Scanner concerns 1–5 cover the 5 new commands; concerns 2 and 3 specifically target the silent-failure response.ReplicationStatus[] and response.PolicyValidationPassed patterns.

vite.notes: 7 new function entries contracted this pass: server.listen (2 postconditions: middleware-mode-forbidden + port-conflict), server.close (1 postcondition: not-awaited), server.restart (1 postcondition: port-conflict), resolveConfig (2 postconditions: config-hook-throws + environments-stripped), loadConfigFromFile (4 postconditions: invalid-loader + not-an-object + esm-only-dependency + tsx-jiti-missing), createBuilder (2 postconditions: config-resolution-failure + environment-init-failure), transformWithOxc (1 postcondition: parse-error). Evidence confirmed from vite@8.x node_modules source (dist/node/chunks/node.js). Key insights: (1) server.listen() throws specifically "Cannot call server.listen in middleware mode." when httpServer is null; (2) loadConfigFromFile() returns null (not throws) when no config file found — only throws on parse errors; (3) createBuilder() initializes all environments in parallel via Promise.all() — first rejection wins; (4) transformWithOxc() attaches .errors array to thrown Error for per-error details.

supertest.omission_reason: The 35 omitted items are: (1) ~34 HTTP verb method names (all uppercase + lowercase variants) on both the request() result object and TestAgent — they all return Test instances whose error profile (AssertionError, ECONNREFUSED, ETIMEDOUT, process hang) is fully covered by the request() and request.agent() postconditions; (2) bearer() is a synchronous method that simply sets a header — no error contract applies. supertest.notes: 1 new function contracted: request.agent() with 4 postconditions (agent-http2-not-supported, agent-cookie-state-leak, agent-server-not-closed, agent-async-unhandled-rejection). Key findings: (1) agent-http2-not-supported throws synchronously in constructor when Node.js http2 module is unavailable — confirmed from agent.js source; (2) agent-cookie-state-leak is the most impactful: shared agent instances cause order-dependent test failures where cookies from one test pollute subsequent tests; (3) agent-server-not-closed is distinct from request() server-not-closed since agents hold server references longer; (4) all 34+ HTTP verb method names intentionally omitted — same Test chain profile as request().

typescript.omission_reason: createWatchProgram/createWatchCompilerHost are incremental build APIs rarely used in SaaS application code. parseCommandLine is a CLI parsing utility. tokenToString/textSpan functions are pure transformations with no throw behavior. createIncrementalProgram follows the same diagnostic model as createProgram. sys.createDirectory has the same ENOENT/EACCES error profile as sys.writeFile already contracted.
typescript.notes: First depth pass (deepen-stream-1 pass 8). 9 new function entries + 1 new postcondition on createProgram = 13 new postconditions total. Key insights: (1) TypeScript Compiler API follows diagnostics-not-throws model — most functions return error arrays rather than throwing; (2) createProgram DOES throw synchronously when string option values are passed instead of enum values (confirmed from typescript.js source line 126937); (3) LanguageService.getSyntacticDiagnostics/getSemanticDiagnostics THROW for unknown filenames via getValidSourceFile() — critical difference from Program.getSyntacticDiagnostics which returns empty array; (4) program.emit and getSemanticDiagnostics throw OperationCanceledException (not extending Error) when cancellation token fires; (5) The canonical compile pattern requires: getPreEmitDiagnostics → check length → emit → check emitSkipped. Skipping any step silently produces broken output.

functions_intentionally_omitted: nock.activate — sync, throws only if already active (nock already active), extremely rare edge case with no distinct behavioral contract
nock.omission_reason: nock.activate() throws if nock is already active, but this is an extremely rare configuration error with no distinct behavioral contract beyond generic Error. All other top-level functions are sync predicates or no-throw utilities.
nock.notes: First depth pass (deepen-stream-2 pass 10). 4 new function entries added. Key insights: (1) nock.load() and loadDefs() use sync fs.readFileSync — throw ENOENT for missing fixture files and SyntaxError for invalid JSON — crashing test setup if unguarded; (2) Scope.done() uses Node assert.ok() — throws AssertionError listing all pending mocks by URL, but calling inside async callbacks causes the error to become an unhandled rejection rather than a test failure; (3) nock.back() throws synchronously (not rejecting) when nock.back.fixtures is null — must be set in beforeAll; (4) nock.back.setMode() throws Error for unknown mode strings — validate against the 5 known BackMode values when reading from env vars. API surface: 9 total contractable functions (8 contracted, 1 intentionally omitted). Effective coverage = 8/9 = 89%.

chai.notes: Re-verification pass 2026-06-12 (deepen-stream-1). chai is a synchronous assertion library — no async/Promise-returning functions exist in the API surface. Validated against chai@6.2.2 (latest). Public exports: Assertion, AssertionError, Should, assert, config, expect, should, use, util. Of these, only 4 are callable with distinct throw contracts: expect, assert, should, assert.ifError. All 4 already contracted with documented postconditions sourced from chaijs.com and chai/index.js. New v6 entry points (register-assert.js / register-expect.js / register-should.js) are sync side-effect imports binding to globalThis — no new throw semantics. use() implementation at index.js:4144 confirmed unchanged — propagates plugin errors synchronously; correctly omitted (sync programming error path only). Bumped last_verified 2026-04-20 → 2026-06-12. Bumped contract_version 1.1.0 → 1.1.1 (no content change, just re-verification). Effective coverage 4/4 = 1.0; raw coverage 4/6 = 0.67 (use and Should are non-error-async sync exports counted in denominator).

(1) authentication.expireAll (2 postconditions: api-error + network-error) — security-critical, used in incident response to revoke all portal tokens; missing try-catch leaves outstanding magic links valid. @sentry/node.notes: 4 new functions contracted. Key insights: (1) startSpanManual — finish()/span.end() not auto-called unlike startSpan; zombie spans from missing finally blocks are the dominant production bug; (2) startInactiveSpan — detached from async context, no auto-cleanup whatsoever; span.end() in finally is the only safe pattern; (3) withMonitor — source-confirmed re-throws after sending error check-in; the silent-miss risk is missing upsertMonitorConfig (slug must pre-exist or be auto-created); (4) captureCheckIn — two-step pattern requires completion check-in in both success and error paths; missing error branch leaves monitor showing 'timed out' instead of 'failed'.

stripe-v21.notes: Pass 1 (2026-06-11): 3 new function contracts added for stripe-node v21 thin event API. isomorphic-git.notes: Pass 5 (deepen-stream-2, 2026-06-12 PM): closed the remaining 7-function gap noted in pass 7's deepen entry. Added addNote, deleteRef, deleteRemote, deleteTag, getRemoteInfo2, remove, removeNote. All error types confirmed by direct throw-site inspection in node_modules/isomorphic-git@1.38.4/index.cjs (line numbers documented in contract.yaml sources). Key insights: (1) addNote is the canonical annotation surface for CI metadata — silent overwrite-prevention via AlreadyExistsError makes the second-build annotation silently lose data unless force:true is set. (2) deleteRef and deleteTag are idempotent on missing refs (fs.rm succeeds silently) — the real error surface is packed-refs lock contention in batch cleanup loops. (3) deleteRemote silently no-ops on missing remotes (deleteSection succeeds) — drift-detection scripts that expect NotFoundError on absent remotes are silently incorrect. (4) getRemoteInfo2 mirrors getRemoteInfo's error surface but is protocol-v2 aware — protocolVersion downgrade to v1 is NOT an error, just a returned field. (5) remove() silently no-ops on missing filepath — typos in pre-commit unstage scripts are the dominant correctness hazard. (6) removeNote silently produces a no-op note commit when the target note doesn't exist — for PII redaction pipelines this is CRITICAL because the cleanup appears to succeed while the sensitive note remains attached. Seven scanner concerns queued (9-15). Effective error-contract surface = 29 contracted / 29 = 1.0 (was 22/29 = 0.76 after pass 7). All 63 isomorphic-git ground-truth tests pass.

esbuild.omission_reason: BuildContext.cancel always resolves (never rejects) — even after dispose(). BuildContext.dispose is idempotent (subsequent calls resolve immediately) — no error contract. buildSync/transformSync/formatMessagesSync/analyzeMetafileSync are synchronous variants that throw synchronously (not async postconditions).
esbuild.notes: Pass 1 (2026-06-15, deepen-stream-2): API surface enumeration from lib/main.d.ts confirmed 7 top-level async functions + 5 BuildContext instance methods = 12 distinct methods. Actual async-callable total set to 13 (counting 4 sync variants for completeness of the ratio, all omitted). Coverage 0/13 raw (0 inherited deepen entries) → 10/13 = 0.77 effective. Key insights: (1) BuildContext.rebuild() has TWO distinct rejection paths: plain Error("The service is no longer running...") after dispose, and BuildFailure from build errors — callers must distinguish these. (2) BuildContext.watch() throws SYNCHRONOUSLY (not async rejection) in non-FS environments — the throw happens before new Promise() is constructed, so .catch() won't intercept it. (3) BuildContext.serve() has 3 distinct failure modes: port-in-use (OS network error), TLS half-config ("Must specify both key and certificate"), and lifecycle violations (disposed/already-enabled). (4) cancel() and dispose() NEVER reject — both check didDispose flag and silently resolve on disposed contexts.
